rulings.Case Title: DANIEL MYTELS VS. AISAR ELVIN KHAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Daniel Mytels Notice Of Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 4, 2018 Line 1, (part 2 of 2) Petitioner Daniel Mytels' Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration) That code section does not require a federal forum. Furthermore, the agreement provides that "[a]rbitration shall be pursuant to the rules of California law." This provision establishes that it is proper for Mr. Mytels to seek confirmation in a California state court per the California Arbitration Act. Mr. Khan's cross-petition to vacate the arbitration award is denied for the same reasons why Mr. Mytels' petition to confirm is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Mytels is required to prepare a proposed statement of decision which conforms to this tentative ruling and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: DANIEL MYTELS VS. AISAR ELVIN KHAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 4, 2018 Line 1, (part 1 of 2) PETITIONER DANIEL MYTELS' Hearing (On Petition To Confirm Arbitration) Petitioner Daniel Mytels' petition to confirm contractual arbitration award is granted. There is no basis to vacate the award under CCP section 1286.2. Respondent Aisar Khan's opposition largely contends that the arbitrator made legal and factual errors, yet California law does not allow for the vacating or correcting of an arbitration award based on errors of law or fact committed by an arbitrator. (Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal. 4th 1.) The court has assumed that the amendment to Mr. Khan's petition to vacate is timely and relates back to his original petition and addresses the merits of his arguments. The Independent Contractor agreement included an arbitration clause and the Joint Defense Agreement did not supersede it. There was no novation. Alexander v. Angel (1951) 37 Cal.2d 856, 862 states that "[w]here it 'satisfactorily appears that a new agreement was intended by the parties to take the place of an existing one,'" a novation exists. There is no language in the Joint Defense Agreement showing that the parties intended to displace their agreement to arbitrate. The record belies Mr. Khan's assertion that Mr. Mytels misled the arbitrator regarding arbitration as she expressly rejected Mr. Khan's position regarding the Joint Defense Agreement and its effect on arbitrability. Mr. Khan's argument that Mr. Mytels needed to assert his claims against Mr. Khan in the underlying Quadri action lacks merit. Mr. Mytels' claim against Mr. Khan for indemnity was not a compulsory cross-claim that was waived by not filing it. A compulsory cross-claim is a claim that needs to be asserted against the plaintiff, not against a co-defendant like Mr. Khan. (CCP § 426.30.) Moreover, the dispute over the indemnity agreement does not arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the main claim and thus does not constitute a "related cause of action" within the meaning of CCP section 426.10(c). There is nothing in the parties' arbitration agreement that suggests that it was not final and binding. The reference to 9 U.S.C. § 1 et seq., which governs resolution of binding arbitration agreements, strongly suggests that the arbitration was supposed to be final and binding. In addition, when Mr. Mytels demanded arbitration pursuant to the binding rules of JAMS, Mr. Khan requested that the matter go to arbitration, which further shows that the parties contemplated binding and final arbitration. Mr. Khan fails to establish that Mr. Mytels needed to file his petition to confirm in federal court. 9 U.S.C. § 9 provides that "If no court is specified in the agreement of the parties, then such application may be made to the United States court in and for the district within which such award was made." (emphasis added.)

rulings.Case Title: COLBY LAM VS. ANDREW KENNY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash Or Modify Plaintiff'S Subpoena Seeking Financial Records From First-Citizens Bank And Trust Company, Including Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, October 4, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANT M-J SF INVESTMENTS, LLC, JAMES NUNEMACHER, THE BANNER DEVELOPMENT COMPANY, A CALIFORNIA, MICHAEL CASSIDY, BRIDGET HARTY-CASSIDY Motion To Quash Or Modify Plaintiff'S Subpoena Seeking Financial Records From First-Citizens Bank And Trust Company, Including Request For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge David Helbraun, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Motion is denied in part granted in part. The subpoena will not be quashed. The requested material is clearly calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible relevant evidence; discovery is broad unless clearly not designed to lead to discovery of admissible relevant evidence, or privileged. The Court addresses moving parties' privacy privilege concerns: the subpoena is hereby modified to exclude personal financial information that was submitted to obtain the loan or financing, based on the moving parties' privacy rights. Defendants do not dispute plaintiff's argument that entities have limited or no privacy rights. The Court rejects defendants' request that they serve as gatekeeper on the requested documents, given their conflict of interest. Sanctions are awarded against moving parties and their counsel of record in the amount of $6,375 because defendants failed to engage in good faith meet and confer and misrepresented the subpoena to the Court in its papers by omitting the important capitalized portion of the subpoena that explicitly states, "NO FINANCIAL INFORMATION OF THE BORROWER(S) IS REQUESTED BY THIS SUBPOENA." Genuine good faith discussion and negotiation between counsel - which would include concrete proposals by the moving parties, the ones seeking relief - likely could have helped defendants to limit the request further than this Court deems necessary as a matter of law. The parties are cautioned that San Francisco Local Rule 2.7(b) requires they submit courtesy copies, and the Court may therefore refuse to hear a motion until courtesy copies are provided sufficiently in advance of the hearing. (END OF PART 1 OF 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SOHNI AHLUWALIA VS. ANNE KERR KENNEDY LHEUREUX ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Sohni Ahluwalia'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Terminating Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities And Declarations Of Plaintiff Sohni Ahluwalia And John A. Holman
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 4, 2018 Line 2, Plaintiff Sohni Ahluwalia'S Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Terminating Sanctions Plaintiff Sohni Ahluwalia's motion to set aside the February 7, 2018 order granting terminating sanctions is granted and she is required to pay defendants $9,582.50 no later than January 1, 2019, but failure to pay is not grounds for reconsideration of this motion. The February 7, 2018 order is now vacated and this case will be restored to the civil active list. Ms. Ahluwalia has shown good cause for granting this motion based on her attendance at the August 17 session of her deposition, her medical condition, and her apparent willingness to work with her counsel in an effort to move forward with this case productively. Granting the motion comports with California's strong policy of deciding cases on their merits and defendants will not suffer legal prejudice by the granting of the motion now that Ms. Ahluwalia has attended another session of her deposition. The amount sought by defendants for the work that their counsel did relating to the events leading up to the February 7 order is both reasonable and comports with the court's own observation of some of that work. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Ahluwalia is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: SHANE STEVENS VS. LLOYD BROWN, DBA LLOYD BROWN SECURITY, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Non-Party Jon Luini To Comply With Subpoena For Production Of Business Records And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 4, 2018 Line 9, Motion To Compel Non-Party Jon Luini To Comply With Subpoena For Production Of Business Records And For Monetary Sanctions Plaintiff Shane Stevens' motion to compel non-party Jon Luini to comply with subpoena for production of business records is granted subject to a protective order covering the information produced by Mr. Luini and Mr. Stevens' request for monetary sanctions is denied. The parties appear to agree that the video that is sought by the subpoena is discoverable. The sole dispute pertains to whether the video should be produced subject to restrictions. Because the video may be subject to contractual rights and obligations of Mr. Luini, so as not to disturb or disrupt those contractual rights and obligations, any party receiving video and other information from Mr. Luini per the subpoena must only use that information for purposes of this lawsuit, must only provide it to persons who have a legitimate good faith need to review the information for purposes of this lawsuit, and must return the information to Mr. Luini at the conclusion of this lawsuit without any copies of the information or any variants of the information being retained by the receiving party. Because the positions taken by Mr. Luini and defendant Big Billy, Inc. were substantially justified, no monetary sanctions are awarded to Mr. Stevens. The ruling on this motion moots Big Billy's motion for protective order, so that motion noticed to be heard on October 22, 2018 at 9am is now off calendar. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Big Billy is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: DON TUCKER ET AL VS. THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company Of Connecticuts Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs To Produce For In Camera Review Documents Withheld On Grounds Of Mediation Privilege
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 4, 2018 Line 5, DEFENDANTs THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT and DIANE FRAZIER's Motion To Compel Plaintiffs To Produce For In Camera Review Documents Withheld On Grounds Of Mediation Privilege Defendants The Travelers Indemnity Company of Connecticut and Diane Frazier's motion to compel plaintiffs to produce for in camera review documents withheld on grounds of privilege is denied. The motion is untimely since it was filed more than 45 days after receiving the last round of responses from plaintiffs Don Tucker and Martin Aguilar to defendants' requests for production of the documents that defendants seek the court's in camera review. Seeking in camera review for the purpose of compelling disclosure if the court's review determines that the documents are not covered by the mediation privilege is, for purposes of the deadline to file a motion to compel, identical in all respects. Because Travelers' positions supporting the filing of this motion do not lack substantial justification, plaintiffs' request for monetary sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: GEORGE RAYMOND SCOTT ET AL VS. WELLS FARGO BANK, NA. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'S Motion To Dismiss Action With Prejudice
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 4, 2018 Line 10, Defendant Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'S Motion To Dismiss Action With Prejudice Defendant Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.'s motion to dismiss action with prejudice is denied. Plaintiffs George and Billie Scott are granted leave to file their second amended complaint no later than October 18, 2018. Denying this motion and granting the Scotts' request to file their second amended complaint comports with California's strong policy favoring deciding cases on their merits and Wells Fargo has not identified any legal prejudice to it if its motion is denied and the Scotts' request is granted. The Scotts' failure to file a timely amended complaint does not warrant depriving them of the ability to pursue their claims. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Scotts is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk
